title: Inspect Unstaged Changes with git diff
summary: Read line-level differences before deciding what belongs in the next commit.

sketchBlocks.title: Inspecting Unstaged Changes with git diff
sketchBlocks.bodyMarkdown:
The `git diff` command is a powerful tool that allows you to see the changes in your working directory that have not yet been staged for commit. This command helps you understand what modifications have been made to your files, so you can decide what to include in your next commit.

Worked example:
Imagine you have a file called `index.html` in your project. You've made some changes to this file, but you're not sure what exactly has been modified. By running `git diff`, you can see a line-by-line comparison of the changes. The output will show lines that have been added, removed, or modified, helping you to review your work before staging it.

For instance, if you added a new paragraph to `index.html`, `git diff` will highlight this addition with a `+` sign at the beginning of the line. If you removed a line, it will be marked with a `-` sign. This visual representation makes it easy to spot changes and ensure that only the intended modifications are included in your commit.

sketchBlocks.title: Interpreting the Diff Output
sketchBlocks.bodyMarkdown:
When you run `git diff`, the output can seem overwhelming at first, but it's structured to help you understand the changes clearly. Each change is prefixed with a header that indicates the file being compared. The lines starting with `---` and `+++` show the old and new file versions, respectively.

The actual changes are displayed with `-` and `+` signs. Lines with a `-` indicate content that was removed, while lines with a `+` show new content. This format allows you to quickly identify what has been added or removed.

Consider a scenario where you have a file `style.css` and you've changed the color scheme. The `git diff` output will show the old color values with `-` and the new ones with `+`. This helps you verify that the changes align with your design intentions before committing.

sketchBlocks.title: Spotting Unrelated Edits
sketchBlocks.bodyMarkdown:
One of the key benefits of using `git diff` is the ability to spot unrelated changes that might have been made unintentionally. This is crucial for maintaining a clean commit history.

Imagine you're working on a feature in `app.js`, but you also made some experimental changes to `config.js` that you don't want to commit yet. By running `git diff`, you can see all the changes across your files. This allows you to selectively stage only the changes related to your feature, leaving the experimental changes for later.

This practice helps in creating focused commits that are easier to understand and review, ensuring that your commit history remains clear and meaningful.

sketchBlocks.title: Using git diff in Your Workflow
sketchBlocks.bodyMarkdown:
Incorporating `git diff` into your workflow can significantly enhance your development process. It allows you to review changes before staging them, ensuring that only the necessary modifications are included in your commits.

For example, if you're working on a collaborative project, using `git diff` before committing can help you communicate your changes more effectively to your team. It ensures that your commits are precise and only contain relevant changes, making code reviews smoother and more efficient.

By regularly using `git diff`, you develop a habit of reviewing your work, which leads to better code quality and a more organized project history.

quizDraft.title: Try It: Inspect Changes in index.html
quizDraft.prompt: You have made changes to `index.html` in your project. Use `git diff` to inspect the unstaged changes. Type the command and press Enter to see the differences.
quizDraft.hint: Use the `git diff` command to view changes in the working directory.

help.concept: The `git diff` command shows changes in the working directory that are not yet staged.
help.hint_1: Ensure you are in the correct directory where `index.html` is located.
help.hint_2: Type `git diff` and press Enter to see the changes.

quizDraft.title: Try It: Review Changes in style.css
quizDraft.prompt: You've updated the color scheme in `style.css`. Use `git diff` to review the changes before staging them. Type the command and press Enter to see the differences.
quizDraft.hint: Use `git diff` to see the changes in `style.css`.

help.concept: `git diff` helps you review changes before staging them.
help.hint_2: Execute `git diff` to view the changes.

quizDraft.title: Try It: Identify Unrelated Changes
quizDraft.prompt: You've made changes to both `app.js` and `config.js`. Use `git diff` to identify unrelated changes that should not be staged together. Type the command and press Enter to see the differences.
quizDraft.hint: Use `git diff` to see changes across multiple files.

help.concept: `git diff` shows changes in all files, helping you identify unrelated edits.
help.hint_1: Ensure both files are in the working directory.
help.hint_2: Run `git diff` to view changes in both files.

quizDraft.title: Try It: Use git diff in a Collaborative Project
quizDraft.prompt: In a collaborative project, you've modified `README.md`. Use `git diff` to ensure your changes are clear and focused before committing. Type the command and press Enter to see the differences.
quizDraft.hint: Use `git diff` to review changes in `README.md`.

help.concept: `git diff` helps ensure changes are clear before committing.
help.hint_2: Execute `git diff` to review the changes.

quizDraft.title: Understanding git diff
quizDraft.prompt: What does the `git diff` command do?
quizDraft.hint: Think about what changes `git diff` shows.

help.concept: `git diff` shows changes in the working directory that are not yet staged.
help.hint_1: `git diff` compares the working directory with the staging area.
help.hint_2: It highlights changes that are not yet staged for commit.

options: Displays changes in the working directory not yet staged

quizDraft.title: Interpreting Diff Output
quizDraft.prompt: Which symbol in `git diff` output indicates a line has been added?
quizDraft.hint: Look for the symbol that represents additions.

help.concept: In `git diff`, a `+` sign indicates an added line.
help.hint_1: The `+` sign is used for additions.

quizDraft.title: Symbol for Removed Lines
quizDraft.prompt: In `git diff`, lines that have been removed are indicated by the symbol `[blank1]`.
quizDraft.hint: Think about the symbol used for deletions.

help.concept: In `git diff`, a `-` sign indicates a removed line.
help.hint_1: The `-` sign is used for deletions.

quizDraft.template: In `git diff`, lines that have been removed are indicated by the symbol `[blank1]`.

quizDraft.title: Purpose of git diff
quizDraft.prompt: The primary purpose of `git diff` is to show changes in the [blank1] that are not yet staged.
quizDraft.hint: Consider where `git diff` looks for changes.

help.concept: `git diff` shows changes in the working directory that are not yet staged.
help.hint_1: `git diff` compares the working directory with the staging area.
help.hint_2: It highlights changes that are not yet staged for commit.

quizDraft.title: Using git diff
quizDraft.hint: Think about when you need to review changes.

help.concept: Use `git diff` to review changes before staging them.

quizDraft.title: Steps to Use git diff
quizDraft.prompt: Arrange the steps to use `git diff` to inspect changes.
quizDraft.hint: Think about the sequence of actions to inspect changes.
